concept: In any right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
why: You'll use this whenever you need to compute diagonal distance — from screen coordinates to physics to 3D graphics.
common_mistakes: Applying the theorem to non-right triangles — it only works when one angle is exactly 90°.
Forgetting to square-root the sum when solving for c.
generate_task: Compute the hypotenuse of a triangle with legs 3 and 4.

quiz.question: If a=6 and b=8, what is c?
The side opposite the right angle is called the ___.
quiz.explanation: The hypotenuse is always the longest side and opposite the 90° angle.

quiz.question: Why does the theorem not apply to obtuse triangles?
quiz.answer: because it assumes a right angle
quiz.explanation: The derivation depends on the 90° angle — for other angles use the law of cosines.

next: Law of cosines — the generalisation to non-right triangles
